title: Mitigation of hydrogen crossover in liquid alkaline water electrolysers using gas recombination catalysts - Nature Energy
gist: Researchers at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory demonstrate that platinum-based cathodes in liquid alkaline water electrolysers (LAWEs) cause drastically elevated hydrogen crossover due to localized hydrogen supersaturation at the cathode-separator interface. A novel Pt-functionalized gas recombination catalyst (GRC) integrated into the Zirfon separator reduces hydrogen crossover by over 95% at 1 A/cm² while maintaining performance for 1,000+ hours, enabling safer use of thinner separators for higher efficiency.

themes.theme: Platinum Cathode Safety Risk in Alkaline Electrolysers

insights.claim: Platinum-based cathodes in liquid alkaline water electrolysers (LAWEs) cause hydrogen-to-oxygen (HTO) ratios exceeding 4.66%, surpassing the lower flammable limit (LFL) of 4% for hydrogen, creating unplanned shutdown risk and cell degradation.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Platinum cathodes — preferred for efficiency — introduce a safety failure mode that must be engineered away before commercial scale-up; this is a non-trivial barrier to deploying high-performance alkaline electrolysers.
insights.quote: LAWEs with a Pt@Ni cathode can potentially exceed the LFL at certain current densities.
insights.locator: Section: Quantifying H2 crossover in LAWEs

insights.claim: Platinum-coated nickel foam cathodes (Pt@Ni) at 0.23 mg/cm² Pt loading reduce cell voltage by 340.8 mV at 2 A/cm² compared with bare nickel foam cathodes, a significant efficiency gain that comes with a hydrogen crossover penalty.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: The efficiency-safety trade-off for platinum cathodes in alkaline electrolysers is now quantified — investors evaluating electrolyser technology should weight both metrics when comparing designs.
insights.quote: The Pt@Ni cathode outperformed the Ni foam cathode (Fig. 2a), achieving a reduction in cell voltage of 340.8 ± 48.8 mV at 2 A cm−2.
insights.locator: Section: Quantifying H2 crossover in LAWEs

insights.claim: The root cause of elevated hydrogen crossover with platinum cathodes is not the material itself but preferential platinum utilization at the cathode-separator (Zirfon) interface, which concentrates hydrogen production in a confined space and drives extreme local supersaturation.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Electrode architecture and placement — not just catalyst composition — are key levers for controlling hydrogen crossover, pointing to engineerable solutions.
insights.quote: Pt@Ni cathode utilization is primarily located at the Zirfon–cathode interface. As a result, drastically higher hydrogen supersaturation is expected to occur at the Zirfon–cathode interface due to discrepancies in electrode utilization.
insights.locator: Section: Evidence and quantification of supersaturation

insights.claim: Moving platinum layers away from the cathode-Zirfon interface to the cathode-flow-field interface produces even lower hydrogen crossover than bare nickel foam cathodes, demonstrating that cathode architecture can independently control the crossover-efficiency trade-off.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Electrode spatial design is an underappreciated lever: companies that optimize platinum placement rather than just loading could achieve both high efficiency and low crossover without requiring additional materials.
insights.quote: LAWEs with a single Pt layer located at the cathode–flow-field interface have an even lower HTO than those with a Ni felt (Fig. 4e) and Ni foam cathode.
insights.locator: Section: How cathode utilization affects LAWE performance and H2 crossover

themes.theme: Gas Recombination Catalyst (GRC) as Safety and Efficiency Enabler

insights.claim: A platinum-functionalized Zirfon separator (Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon) reduces hydrogen crossover by over 95% at 1 A/cm² in LAWEs without degrading separator ionic conductivity or cell electrochemical performance.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: A 95%+ crossover reduction eliminates the primary safety barrier to using high-efficiency platinum cathodes in alkaline electrolysers, potentially unlocking thinner-separator designs for higher system efficiency.
insights.quote: At each investigated current density, Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon provided a high recombination efficacy of over 95%.
insights.locator: Section: Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon LAWE performance and recombination efficacy

insights.claim: The Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon GRC reduces hydrogen yield loss to under 0.1%, meaning the recombination of crossover hydrogen has negligible impact on overall electrolyser hydrogen output.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: GRC integration does not materially reduce hydrogen production economics — the loss is negligible at <0.1% of hydrogen yield, making GRC adoption commercially viable.
insights.quote: this result shows that the loss in hydrogen yield due to the GRC is less than 0.1%
insights.locator: Section: Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon LAWE performance and recombination efficacy

insights.claim: The Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon separator also improves LAWE cell voltage by 74 mV compared to pristine Zirfon, attributed to the enhanced oxygen evolution reaction (OER) activity of platinum nanoparticles on the anode surface — a bonus performance gain from the GRC.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: The GRC provides a dual benefit — safety and efficiency — making the economics of platinum-functionalized separators more compelling than a pure safety-mitigation add-on.
insights.quote: LAWEs with Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon outperformed those using pristine Zirfon (Fig. 6a), with a voltage reduction of 74 ± 18 mV.
insights.locator: Section: Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon LAWE performance and recombination efficacy

insights.claim: Nafion binder-based GRC designs (as used in PEM electrolysers) are incompatible with alkaline electrolysers because Nafion has low anionic conductivity, causing performance losses; LAWE-specific binder-free GRC designs are required.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: PEM-derived GRC technology cannot be directly transplanted to alkaline electrolysers — separate IP and process development is needed, representing a differentiation opportunity for alkaline-focused companies.
insights.quote: When Pt nanoparticles mixed with Nafion binder as the GRC were coated on the anode side of the Zirfon surface ... performance loss was observed due to high Ohmic loss, likely induced by the low anionic conductivity of the binder.
insights.locator: Section: Mitigating crossover via GRC

themes.theme: Hydrogen Supersaturation: Direct Evidence and Quantification

insights.claim: Dissolved hydrogen concentration near the cathode in LAWEs can be one to three orders of magnitude higher than the hydrogen solubility limit, as quantified by in-situ RRDE and in-cell platinum probe measurements — direct experimental proof of hydrogen supersaturation in operating electrolysers.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Supersaturation is far larger than previously assumed and is the dominant driver of crossover flux; models that used solubility-only assumptions underestimate hydrogen safety risk at the system level.
insights.quote: The results show that c_H2^Ca is one to three orders of magnitude higher than the hydrogen solubility.
insights.locator: Section: Evidence and quantification of supersaturation

insights.claim: Hydrogen supersaturation during HER in LAWEs can be directly measured in operando using a platinum wire probe inserted between the cathode and separator, providing a new diagnostic tool for cell-level crossover characterization.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: This measurement approach is transferable to other electrolyser designs and could become a standardized diagnostic for safety qualification, enabling more rigorous testing protocols.
insights.quote: A HOR current was measured in the absence of a water electrolysis current (Fig. 3g) due to hydrogen bubbling to ensure hydrogen saturation in the bulk catholyte. A higher HOR current was observed on applying an electrolysis current (Fig. 3g), indicating that the hydrogen concentration in the electrolyte passes the solubility limit and reaches supersaturation.
insights.locator: Section: Evidence and quantification of supersaturation

insights.claim: Electrolyte mixing without degassing increases hydrogen crossover in LAWEs, but when industrial-grade degassing is applied before mixing, hydrogen crossover under mixing conditions becomes identical to non-mixing conditions, confirming that engineered degassing fully controls mixing-related crossover.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Well-designed industrial degassing systems effectively eliminate one source of crossover, shifting the residual risk entirely to intrinsic transport processes that require separator or catalyst solutions.
insights.quote: with degassing, identical HTO values were observed under mixing and non-mixing conditions ... degassing effectively suppresses the hydrogen crossover associated with electrolyte mixing.
insights.locator: Section: Quantifying H2 crossover in LAWEs

insights.claim: Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon maintained stable crossover mitigation efficacy and cell performance for over 1,000 hours at 1 A/cm² continuous operation, with post-test HTO profiles nearly unchanged from beginning-of-life measurements.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: 1,000-hour stability at industrial current density brings GRC-enabled alkaline electrolysers closer to commercial readiness; durability has historically been a sticking point for novel separator technologies.
insights.quote: The cell performance remained stable for the testing period after an initial improvement (Fig. 7a) ... After the durability test, Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon still provided a high recombination efficacy, as evidenced by an almost unchanged HTO profile.
insights.locator: Section: Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon durability in LAWEs

insights.claim: Platinum oxidation at the anode surface of Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon occurs predominantly in the initial operation period and does not scale linearly with time — metallic platinum fraction stabilizes, preserving GRC activity for long-term operation.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Initial Pt oxidation is a self-limiting process, not a progressive degradation mechanism, which is a critical design insight supporting long-duration deployment without periodic regeneration.
insights.quote: The similar changes in the fraction of metallic Pt observed after 200 and 1,000 h indicate that Pt oxidation does not scale linearly with operation time, but instead predominantly occurs during the initial stages.
insights.locator: Section: Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon durability in LAWEs

insights.claim: Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon also demonstrated durability under 2,000-cycle dynamic operation-shutdown conditions, with stable polarization curves and HTO profiles post-cycling, demonstrating suitability for variable renewable energy inputs.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Alkaline electrolysers paired with GRC-modified separators appear compatible with intermittent renewable power, expanding their addressable market to solar/wind-coupled green hydrogen production.
insights.quote: Comparison of the polarization curves and HFR and HTO profiles of the BoL and post-cycled cells further confirmed the operational durability of Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon and indicated its versatility for dynamic operation.
insights.locator: Section: Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon durability in LAWEs

themes.theme: Zirfon Separator Thinning as Efficiency Pathway

insights.claim: Thinner Zirfon diaphragms (50–100 µm vs. the standard 220–500 µm) would reduce Ohmic resistance and improve LAWE efficiency, but previously caused unacceptable hydrogen crossover — GRC technology now provides a pathway to deploy thinner separators safely.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: If validated at scale, GRC-enabled thin-separator designs could materially improve alkaline electrolyser efficiency and reduce the cost of green hydrogen, benefiting companies across the alkaline electrolyser supply chain.
insights.quote: The results of our study imply that further reducing the thickness of the Zirfon separator could boost efficiency while ensuring safe operation in LAWEs.
insights.locator: Section: Conclusions

insights.claim: The standard Zirfon UTP 220 separator (220 µm, up to 75% porosity) is currently the industry default for LAWEs, but its thickness and associated Ohmic resistance are constraints on achievable efficiency — improvements require either geometry changes or novel separator functionalization.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Agfa-Gevaert's Zirfon has an entrenched position in alkaline electrolysers, but separator functionalization and geometry innovation are emerging competitive dimensions — this creates opportunities for both Agfa and potential disruptors.
insights.quote: One of the most widely used separators is the Zirfon diaphragm with a typical thickness of ~220 or 500 μm and a porosity of up to 75%
insights.locator: Introduction

themes.theme: DOE Targets and Cost Economics of Alkaline Electrolysis

insights.claim: The US Department of Energy's 2026 target for alkaline electrolysers is 1.0 A/cm² at 1.8 V per cell — a benchmark that requires platinum-group metal catalysts on cathodes since standard nickel cathodes cannot meet this performance metric.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: DOE 2026 targets effectively mandate high-activity cathode catalysts (Pt-based), making safety solutions like GRC not optional but necessary for regulatory and programmatic compliance.
insights.quote: more active electrodes than the widely used nickel (Ni) are desired to meet advanced performance metrics (for instance, US Department of Energy 2026 target: 1.0 A cm−2 at 1.8 V per cell)
insights.locator: Introduction

insights.claim: Electricity price is the dominant cost driver for alkaline water electrolysis, making cell voltage reduction through better cathode catalysts (such as Pt-based HER electrodes) the highest-leverage cost reduction lever.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Efficiency gains from Pt cathodes directly translate to lower levelized hydrogen cost — quantifying this linkage helps investors prioritize electrolyser technology investments on voltage efficiency rather than capex alone.
insights.quote: As electricity prices drive water electrolysis costs, the levelized cost of produced hydrogen can be reduced using a Pt cathode with appropriate loading.
insights.locator: Introduction

insights.claim: The GRC Pt loading on the Zirfon separator is only 0.062 mg/cm², a very low loading level designed to balance crossover mitigation performance with platinum cost — limiting the material cost penalty of GRC adoption.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Low platinum loading in the GRC reduces material cost sensitivity to platinum price fluctuations — a commercially attractive property for scale-up.
insights.quote: XRF mapping revealed a uniform Pt in-plane distribution with a Pt loading of 0.062 ± 0.004 mg cm−2
insights.locator: Section: Design and characterization of Pt-GRCAn-Zirfon

themes.theme: Transferability and Broader Electrochemical Device Implications

insights.claim: Gas recombination catalyst design principles developed for alkaline water electrolysers can be extended to other electrochemical devices where gas crossover is a safety or efficiency concern, broadening the technology's addressable market.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: The GRC approach is not LAWE-specific — technology transfer to other electrochemical platforms (fuel cells, CO2 electrolysers) is a potential value expansion vector for any company commercializing this approach.
insights.quote: Similar catalyst design principles can be extended to many emerging electrochemical devices for the mitigation of gas crossover.
insights.locator: Section: Conclusions

insights.claim: GRCs were previously only implemented in proton exchange membrane water electrolysers (PEMWEs) and have now, for the first time, been successfully developed for liquid alkaline water electrolysers, filling a critical technology gap.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: This represents a first-mover advantage opportunity for alkaline electrolyser developers that can license or replicate this GRC fabrication approach before competitors.
insights.quote: to our knowledge, GRCs have not been developed for use in LAWEs.
insights.locator: Introduction

insights.claim: A previously unreported phenomenon is that hydrogen is chemically consumed at the nickel anode before GC measurement — existing LAWE hydrogen crossover measurements in the literature may systematically understate actual crossover fluxes.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Published safety data for alkaline electrolysers may understate hydrogen crossover, meaning real-world safety margins could be tighter than assumed — a concern for operators and regulators scaling up LAWE deployment.
insights.quote: the observed discrepancy corresponds to a lower measured hydrogen crossover, meaning that a certain amount of hydrogen is consumed at the anode before being measured by gas chromatography
insights.locator: Section: Quantifying H2 crossover in LAWEs

themes.theme: Verdagy and Industrial Electrode Supply Chain

insights.claim: Commercial alkaline electrolyser company Verdagy supplied industrial-grade electrode materials used in the 1,000-hour durability test, providing a direct link between laboratory GRC research and industrial-scale electrode technology.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Verdagy's collaboration with Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory on GRC research suggests the startup is engaged with next-generation safety and performance technologies — relevant for investors monitoring its development roadmap.
insights.quote: The electrodes used for durability tests were provided by Verdagy.
insights.locator: Section: Chemicals and materials
